aliases: 薯条中份 (Shǔ Tiáo Zhōng Fèn)

chinese: 薯条中份 (Shǔ Tiáo Zhōng Fèn)

region_of_origin: USA (McDonald's global product); Singapore market variant
food_subgroup: Deep-fried potato strips; fast food side dish; medium serving ~114g

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for commercial french fries; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium serving of McDonald's French Fries in Singapore is approximately 114 grams, providing around 337 kcal, 44g carbohydrate, 15g fat, and 230mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: McDonald's French Fries Medium — McDonald's Singapore Official Nutritional Data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from McDonald's Singapore official nutrition page for the medium French Fries serving. Per-100g values back-calculated from the 114g medium serving data. McDonald's Singapore is the brand owner and primary data authority for this specific commercial product in the Singapore market. Values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central entry for fast food french fries (USDA SR Legacy #21137) for plausibility validation; values are consistent.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried potato / french fries
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 for fried potato strips is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database range for french fries (GI 63–75, reference food glucose=100). The dextrose (glucose) coating applied to McDonald's fries raises the effective GI above plain fried potato (GI ~63). GI 72 is used as a defensible mid-range estimate for dextrose-coated commercial french fries. GL per medium serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 44.0) / 100 = 31.7. Peer-reviewed basis: Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 230mg per medium serving (201.8mg/100g); cumulative meal sodium risk when ordered as part of a combo
hypertension.guidance: A medium serving of McDonald's French Fries contributes 230mg sodium — moderate in isolation — but when combined with a burger (e.g., McSpicy at ~1,040mg sodium), the total combo meal sodium reaches ~1,270mg, representing over half the WHO daily limit of 2,000mg; hypertensive patients should avoid combo meals and limit fries to occasional small servings.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) and high glycemic load (~31.7 per medium serving) due to dextrose coating and gelatinised potato starch
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of ~72 and GL of ~31.7 per medium serving, McDonald's French Fries pose a high glycaemic risk for T2DM patients; consumption should be avoided or strictly limited to the smallest available portion (small fries ~79g, GL ~22), paired with a high-protein food to attenuate glucose response, and never upsized.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 13.2g/100g (15g per serving), saturated fat 1.8g/100g, ultra-processed deep-fried product with acrylamide formation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of deep-fried commercial french fries is associated with increased cardiovascular risk due to total fat load, oxidised frying oils, and acrylamide (a probable carcinogen formed during high-temperature frying of starchy foods); CVD patients should limit intake to occasional consumption and prioritise lower-fat potato preparations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Acrylamide formation during deep-frying (estimated 500–600 mcg/kg in commercial fries); pro-inflammatory dietary pattern
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-frying generates acrylamide (IARC Group 2A probable carcinogen) with pro-inflammatory associations in vitro; RA patients should limit french fry consumption to no more than twice monthly and prioritise anti-inflammatory dietary patterns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: McDonald's French Fries (Medium, Singapore market) are deep-fried potato strips served in a medium portion of approximately 114 grams, providing around 337 kcal, 4g protein, 15g fat (1.8g saturated), 44g carbohydrate, and 230mg sodium per serving. The fries are made from par-fried frozen potato strips coated with dextrose (glucose) before final deep-frying in vegetable oil, which gives them their characteristic golden colour, crispy exterior, and fluffy interior. The dextrose coating raises the glycemic index above that of plain fried potato to approximately GI 72 (HIGH), resulting in a glycemic load of ~31.7 per medium serving — equivalent to the glycaemic impact of approximately 2 bowls of white rice when consumed as part of a value meal. McDonald's Singapore fries are certified halal, vegan, and gluten-free. They are a ubiquitous fast food side dish and snack, commonly ordered as part of combo meals. From a clinical nutrition perspective, the high GI and GL make them unsuitable for T2DM patients in medium or large portions; the cumulative sodium from a combo meal (burger + fries) can exceed 1,270mg, which is a significant concern for hypertensive patients. Acrylamide content (~500–600 mcg/kg) is a concern for inflammatory conditions including rheumatoid arthritis. Data sourced from McDonald's Singapore official nutritional data, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central for plausibility.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from McDonald's Singapore official nutritional data (brand owner, exact product). Per-100g values back-calculated from the 114g medium serving. GI value of 72 is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database range for commercial fried potato strips and is supported by peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). All core macros and secondary nutrients are fully populated from the primary source.
notes: trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per McDonald's Singapore official nutritional data — McDonald's Singapore reformulated frying oils to eliminate artificial trans fats. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per McDonald's Singapore official data; potato-based product fried in vegetable oil contains no dietary cholesterol. sugar_g is confirmed zero per McDonald's Singapore official data; the dextrose coating is metabolised as glucose but is reported as 0g sugar on the nutrition panel (below rounding threshold per Singapore food labelling regulations). vitamin_a_mcg_rae is confirmed zero; vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero; vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero — these are consistent with a plant-based fried potato product. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown is not reported on the McDonald's Singapore nutrition panel and is not available in the Singapore HPB database for this specific commercial product. water_g is set to -1: moisture content is not reported by McDonald's Singapore and is not available from the primary source for this specific product formulation. Selenium_mcg value of 0.2 per 100g is carried from the input record; this is a plausible trace amount for fried potato and is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for fast food french fries. The medium serving size of 114g is used as the canonical Singapore market portion for GL calculation; the input record also lists 117g as an alternative verified portion — the 114g figure is used as it is the McDonald's Singapore-stated medium serving weight. Acrylamide content is not a standard nutrition panel nutrient but is clinically relevant for this food; estimated 500–600 mcg/kg based on published studies of commercial french fries.
